---
title: "Digital Markets and Platforms"
---

## Learning goals
- Analyze platform competition, self-preferencing, algorithmic effects, and data advantages.
- Model indirect network effects and multi-homing; assess default position power.
- Integrate product design evidence with empirical outcomes.

## Core topics
- Platform demand: multi-sided participation, steering, and matching efficiency.
- Self-preferencing and ranking: click-through impacts; A/B test interpretation.
- Data advantages and economies of scope; switching costs and lock-in.
- Algorithmic pricing and collusion risks.

::: {.callout-note title="Method box"}
- Platform price/fee pass-through; elasticity across sides.
- Event studies on default changes or ranking tweaks.
- Simple two-sided logit/bargaining sketches; diversion across sides.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Qualitative evidence"}
- Product requirement docs and experiments; governance and content policies.
- Partner contracts (parity/MFN, exclusivity), API access terms.
- User research surveys on multi-homing and switching.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Citations and comparative note"}
- Cite platform cases and regulations: US (e.g., Epic v. Apple, DOJ Google Search/Ad Tech), EU (Google Shopping/Android, DMA), UK (CMA market investigations), and other jurisdictions (e.g., Korea KFTC app stores where relevant).
- Reference empirical studies on defaults and ranking effects, and any A/B or natural experiment evidence from platforms.
- Note differences in treatment of MFNs/parity and self-preferencing across jurisdictions.
:::
